coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots functionalized silver nanoparticles for the colorimetric detection of phoxim
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Mingda
Wang, Chenge
Wang, Yingying
Wei, Wei
Ma, Shuang
Sun, Xiaohan
He, Jiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, Lycii Fructus as raw materials for green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) reduce AgNO3. The CDs-AgNPs were synthesized by one-step method. CDs were applied to stabilize AgNPs due to abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs. In presence of phoxim, the dispersed CDs-AgNPs get aggregated and the absorption peak with red shift from 400 nm to 525 nm, resulting in the color changed from yellow to red. Under optimized conditions, the absorbance ratio at A525 nm/A400 nm was related linearly to the concentrations of phoxim in the range of 0.1–100 μM. The detection limit was calculated to 0.04 μM, which is lower than maximum residue limits of phoxim in samples in China. The colorimetric sensor was successfully utilized to monitoring phoxim in environmental and fruit samples with good recoveries ranges from 87% to 110.0%. These results showed the sensor had a promising application prospect in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lycii Fructus
CDs-AgNPs
Phoxim
Colorimetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for spectrophotometric detection of phoxim
Analysis of phoxim in environmental samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a XRD of CDs and b FT-IR spectra of (a) CDs, (b) CDs-AgNPs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs-AgNPs.
DLS data of a CDs, b CDs-Ag NPs, c the aggregation of CDs-AgNPs induced by phoxim. TEM images of d CDs, e CDs-Ag NPs and f the aggregation of CDs-AgNPs induced by phoxim.
UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-AgNPs before and after addition of phoxim.
Absorbance ratio of A525 nm/A400 nm of CDs-AgNPs solution with different concentrations of CDs range from 0.01 mg/kg to 0.30 mg/kg in presence of phoxim.
a UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-AgNPs at different reaction time in presence of phoxim, b Time-absorbance ratio of A525 nm/A400 nm.
The influences of buffer solution pH value.

a The corresponding photograph of CDs-AgNPs with different concentrations of phoxim, b UV–vis spectral changes of CDs-AgNPs solutions upon addition of different concentrations of phoxim (0.1 μM to 100 μM) at buffer PH= 6.0 and c calibration curve of CDs-AgNPs (A525 nm/A400 nm) against log10 of different concentrations of phoxim (0.1 μM to 100 μM).
The normalized absorbance change of CDs-AgNPs in one month.

a UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs-AgNPs in presence of phoxim, b Absorbance ratio (A525 nm/A400 nm) of the CDs-AgNPs solutions before and after addition of phoxim in presence of different ions and pesticides. phoxim: 3.0 × 10−5 M, cations, anions, pesticides: 3.0 × 10−5 M.
Schematic representation for the colorimetric detection of phoxim using CDs-AgNPs as a colormetric probe.
Comparison of this work with other previous methods for the detection of TM.
Analytical results for Phoxim in environmental samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots functionalized silver nanoparticles for the colorimetric detection of phoxim

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, Lycii Fructus as raw materials for green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) reduce AgNO3. The CDs-AgNPs were synthesized by one-step method. CDs were applied to stabilize AgNPs due to abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs. In presence of phoxim, the dispersed CDs-AgNPs get aggregated and the absorption peak with red shift from 400 nm to 525 nm, resulting in the color changed from yellow to red. Under optimized conditions, the absorbance ratio at A525 nm/A400 nm was related linearly to the concentrations of phoxim in the range of 0.1–100 μM. The detection limit was calculated to 0.04 μM, which is lower than maximum residue limits of phoxim in samples in China. The colorimetric sensor was successfully utilized to monitoring phoxim in environmental and fruit samples with good recoveries ranges from 87% to 110.0%. These results showed the sensor had a promising application prospect in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate pesticides (OPs) belong to one branch of organic pesticides [1,2]. OPs are the most widely used in both agricultural and domestic gardens, especially in developing countries [3]. Phoxim (O,O-Diethyl-O-α-Oximinophenyl cyanophosphorothioate), a broad spectrum antiparasite, is an effective insecticide for the control of pests [4–6]. However, excessive residues of phoxim not only can pose threat to the environment and human health, leading to the contamination of lakes and rivers but also affect the food consumption, growth and reproduction [7,8]. Meanwhile, phoxim also inhibits acetylcholinesterase activity and oxidative stress of B. mori [9]. To realize a simple, selective and on-situ detection, a variety of methods have been developed for the detection of phoxim. Such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [12], enzymatic kinetic method [13], amperometric biosensor, and electrochemical analysis (EC) [14] have been proposed for the detection of phoxim in various environmental samples. Despite a number of analytical methods, most of them require tedious procedures, sophisticated instrumentations, and harmful reagents, which limit their desired and promising application. Therefore, reliable, simple and inexpensive method with good selectivity and sensitivity are still in highly demanded to detect phoxim in environmental samples.

At present, a variety of colorimetric sensors have drawn extensive attention due to simplicity, low cost and sensitivity [15,16]. Especially metal nanoparticles including gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were designed due to their unique optical and physico-chemical properties [17–20]. As a result, AuNPs and AgNPs have been utilized as colorimetric sensors for the detection of inorganic and organic molecules with high sensitivity and selectivity [21–24]. For example, Zhang group [25] developed that nucleotide-capped AgNPs as a sensor to detect D-cysteine and L-cysteine. Yang et al. [26] designed smaller etched AgNPs as a rapid sensor for the detection of Al3+ in real water. Our group [27] illustrated the utility of thioglycolic acid capped silver nanoparticles (TGA-AgNPs) as the visual sensor for the detection of 6-benzylaminopurine (6-BAP) in environmental samples. These reports demonstrated that AuNPs and AgNPs-based colorimetric sensors provide simple and effective method for on-situ monitoring different analytes with high sensitivity and selectivity. However, green synthesis of CDs functionalized nanomaterials are not attracted extensive attentions by researchers. Therefore, green synthesis of CDs-based AgNPs can provide a promising alternative for colorimetric sensing organic pesticides.

Carbon dots (CDs) are attractive family nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm. They offer certain advantages as interesting photophysical and chemical properties, including low toxicity, resistance to photobleaching, high fluorescence brightness and biocompatibility [28,29]. Due to these excellent properties of CDs, they have promising potential applications in various fields. Recently, most researchers regard CDs as reducing and stabilizing agents for synthesis of metallic nanoparticles as sensors for applications. For example, Amjadi et al. [30] have exploited carbon quantum dot/silver nanocomposite as colorimetric sensor for assay of methimazole. Shi and coworkers [31] have developed nitrogen-doped graphene quantumdot-silver nanoparticle for visual detection of dihydroxybenzene isomers. Despite distinct disadvantages of these researches, they need chemical reagents rather than natural materials. Here, Lycii Fructus was chosen as a precursor to synthesize fluorescent CDs with sufficient functional groups on the surface such as -NH2, -OH and -COOH.

In this study, green synthesis of CDs-modified AgNPs using CDs as stabilizing agents and NaBH4 as a reducing agent. In presence of phoxim, the dispersed CDs-modified AgNPs would get aggregated, yielding the color changes from yellow to red. Based on the phenomenon, a novel and simple colorimetric sensor for monitoring phoxim in environmental samples has been developed.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was provided Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3) and acetic acid (CH3COOH) were received from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Co. Ltd (Tianjin, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and phoxim were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used without further purification. Milli-Q-purified distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a TU-1901 UV–visible spectrometer (Beijing, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a Tecnai G2F30 instrument (FEI, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by a Nicolet NEXUS 670 spectrophotometer (Nicolet USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded by X′Pert Diffractometer (Philips Netherlands). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) were performed on Zetasizer Nano BI-200SM instrument (Brookhaven, USA).

The green fluorescent CDs were synthesized according to reported method in our group [32]. Briefly, 0.5 g Lycii Fructus were grinded and added 15 mL distilled water. The solution was filtered by vacuum to obtain clarified solution, the above solution and 0.5 mL ammonia solution (25%) were put into pressure vessel and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product was filtered by 0.22 μM membrane. Finally, the brown-yellow solution of CDs was dried by vacuum oven at 60 °C.

CDs-capped AgNPs were prepared by reduction of AgNO3 with NaBH4 in presence of CDs at room temperature. In this method, AgNPs (0.5 mL, 20 mM) and CDs (20 mL, 0.1 mg/mL) solutions were added into 250 mL reaction flask containing 20 mL B-R buffer with pH 6.0. Then the solution was stirred for 30 min and 1 mL freshly prepared NaBH4 (15 mM) was added dropwise into the reaction flask. The solution was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The obtained deep yellow solution indicated the CDs-AgNPs was successfully synthesized. The solution was highly stable and no precipitation for several months in refrigerator at 4.0 ± 2.0 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for spectrophotometric detection of phoxim
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for detection of phoxim was as follows. 500 µL CDs-AgNPs solution, 500 µL B-R buffer solution and 1 mL were added into a 4-mL Cuvette. Then, 100 µL different concentrations of phoxim (0.1–150 µM) were added and incubated for 10 min. The color changes were observed with naked eyes and spectra were recorded on UV–vis spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of phoxim in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and Yellow River were collected from Lanzhou city. They were filtered by 0.22 µm membrane and spiked with different concentrations of phoxim (0.1–150 µM).

The fruit sample (apple) was purchased from local supermarket in Lanzhou. The extraction procedure was carried out by reported method [33]. The fruit sample was rinsed with distilled water and ultrapure water, respectively. After drying, 2 g of apple was chopped and spiked with various concentrations of phoxim. Then the mixture was sonicated for 5 min and filtered by 0.22 µm. Finally, the obtained solutions were stored in refrigerator at 4.0 ± 2.0 °C.

In this work, the prepared CDs were characterized by XRD, UV–vis and FT-IR spectroscopy. The XRD of CDs had an evident peak at 2θ = 22° in Fig. 1, which suggested the CDs were successfully synthesized [34]. The functional groups of CDs were also revealed by FT-IR (Fig. 1b), peaks of 3377 cm−1 and 3224 cm−1 stand for the stretching vibrations of -OH and -NH2, the peak at 2930 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C-H. The stretching vibration of C˭C and C˭O appears at 1630 cm−1. Peaks of 1593 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 are assigned to vibrations of C˭N and C-N. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs-AgNPs revealed absorption bands at 3437 cm−1, 1725 cm−1 1582 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 corresponding to O-H, C˭O, C˭N and C-N bonds, respectively, which proved the CDs-AgNPs were successfully synthesized. Therefore, these functional groups of CDs-AgNPs could interact with phoxim.

UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs-AgNPs were showed in Fig. 2. CDs exhibit an evident band [35] at 271 nm, which attributed to n-π* transition of C˭O. The color of The CDs was close to colorless. CDs-AgNPs had a typical absorption peak at 400 nm, which indicated CDs-AgNPs were synthesized by chemical reduction. The color of CDs functionalized AgNPs was deep yellow (inset Fig. 2).

The morphology and size of CDs, dispersed CDs-AgNPs, and aggregated CDs-AgNPs were characterized by DLS. As indicated in Fig. 3a, the CDs had an average diameter at 4 nm, wheras the CDs-AgNPs showed the average hydrodynamic diameter at 7 nm (Fig. 3b), in presence of phoxim (30 μM), the average diameter became larger from 7 nm to 83 nm (Fig. 3c), indicating the phoxim could aggregate the CDs-AgNPs.

The shape of CDs, dispersed CDs-AgNPs, and aggregated CDs-AgNPs were also examined by TEM. The CDs (Fig. 3d) and CDs-AgNPs (Fig. 3e) were uniform and well-dispersed, which consistent with the results of DLS. These results indicate that CDs and CDs-AgNPs were successfully synthesized. Fig. 3f represented the TEM image of CDs-AgNPs in presence of 30 μM phoxim, the CDs-AgNPs got irregular and larger, which indicating phoxim could interact with CDs-AgNPs and aggregated.

The mechanism of this work is presented in Scheme 1. The as-prepared CDs-AgNPs were well-dispersed deep yellow solution. The CDs contain a large number of nitrogen-containing (-NH2) and oxygen-containing (-OH and -COOH) groups which can strongly interact with analytes. The -NH2 groups would absorb on the surface of prepared silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Meanwhile, the free -NH2, -OH and -COOH functional groups would stabilize the CDs-AgNPs relying on repulsive electrostatic forces. Phoxim contains many cyano and ethoxy groups. In acidic conditions, the CDs-AgNPs would obtain negative charges because of pKa of -NH2 and -COOH on the surface of CDs, at the same time, these groups of phoxim (pKa=4.39) obtain positive charges which tend to interact with free amino and carboxylate groups to form coordination complex via electrostatic interactions. Therefore, the CDs-AgNPs became cross-linked via phoxim, which resulted in the aggregation of CDs-AgNPs and color changed from yellow to red with red shift of absorption peak from 400 nm to 525 nm. These changes could be seen through the spectra and photograph of CDs-AgNPs before and after addition of phoxim ( Fig. 4). Other similar or co-existing structures of pesticides could not lead to the aggregation of CDs-AgNPs.

In this work, green synthesis of CDs had many functional groups including hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups which strongly interact with phoxim. Therefore, the amount of CDs plays an important role in quantitative and qualitative detection of phoxim. To investigate the effect of CDs concentration, different amount of CDs were added into solution for modification in presence of phoxim (30 µM). Fig. 5 revealed that when the concentration of CDs below 1.0 mg/kg, the analytical signal (A525 nm/A400 nm) was low, which means the degree of aggregation was weak. When the concentration exceed 1.0 mg/kg, decrease in A525 nm/A400 nm was probably due to excessive CDs would interact with phoxim which.reduce the aggregation of CDs-AgNPs. So, 1.0 mg/kg was employed for the determination of phoxim.

Reaction time is also an important factor to detect analytes in experiments. Therefore, the analytical signal (A525 nm/A400 nm) of CDs-AgNPs with phoxim (30 µM) was evaluated within 30 min. As represented in Fig. 6, before 10 min, the signals rise rapidly, due to a small amount of phoxim can be fully integrated with the CDs-AgNPs. The signals kept stable after 10 min, the result showed CDs-AgNPs completely interacted with phoxim. Thus, 10 min was used in this assay.

The as-prepared CDs-AgNPs were examined different pH levels ranged from 2 to 10. ΔA represented A525 nm/A400 nm in presence of phoxim (30 μM) withdrawing the background, which showed the aggregation degree of CDs-AgNPs. A higher ΔA revealed the higher aggregation degree of CDs-AgNPs. As depicted in Fig. 7, it revealed that the ΔA rapidly increased with the pH from 2.0 to 6.0 probably due to the partial protonation of functional groups on the surface of CDs had weaker interaction with phoxim resulting in incomplete aggregation of CDs-AgNPs. However, when the pH value varied from 7.0 to 10.0, ΔA gradually decreased, it is likely that functional groups on the surface of CDs maybe deprotonated at alkaline conditions, resulting in the electrostatic force decreased, thus made aggregation of CDs-AgNPs. Therefore, pH= 6.0 was carried out for the whole experiments.

Under the optimized conditions, absorption spectra of CDs-AgNPs were measured for quantification of phoxim, the color changes could be observed by naked eyes with different concentrations of phoxim. As shown in Fig. 8a, with increasing concentrations of phoxim, the color of CDs-AgNPs gradually changed from yellow to red. wheras resulting in the SPR peak of CDs-AgNPs had a red shift from 400 to 525 nm (Fig. 8b). The absorbance ratio (A525 nm/A400 nm) was also established to reveal the molar ratio of dispersed/aggregated CDs-AgNPs. To quantitatively detect phoxim, the absorbance ratio (A525 nm/A400 nm) and the range of 0.1–100 μM of phoxim showed a linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.988 (Fig. 8c). The limit of detection (LOD) (S/N ratio =3) was calculated to be 0.04 μM, which lower than the maximum concentration of phoxim (0.17 μM) in food samples in China [36]. Table 1 represented the comparative data between reported analytical techniques and the performance of proposed sensor for the detection of phoxim. Clearly, the fabricated CDs-AgNPs, as a colorimetric sensor, had high sensitivity, a good linearity and suitable simplicity for determination of phoxim.

To ensure the reproductivity of CDs-AgNPs towards phoxim (30 μM), the stability of CDs-AgNPs was examined. As shown in Fig. 9, the material was measured by UV–vis spectrometer for a month under optimal conditions, the result showed that CDs-AgNPs probe could remain stable, which indicated that the novel CDs-AgNPs probe had high stability.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of CDs-AgNPs towards phoxim, some common cations, anions and pesticides were investigated in presence of phoxim. The UV–vis of absorption spectra of CDs-AgNPs can be observed in presence of interferences ( Fig. 10a). The assay was put forward by measuring the A525 nm/A400 nm of the CDs-AgNPs probe for detecting 30 μM phoxim with various co-existing ions and pesticides, including cations (Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+, 1.0 mM), anions (NO3 -, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, Cl-, Br-, S2O3 2-, CH3COO-, 1.0 mM), pesticides (chlorothalonil, glyphosate, triazolone, parathion, carbendazim, 30 μM), respectively. These results revealed that these interferences including possible co-existing pesticides did not induce the aggregation of CDs-AgNPs. After addition of phoxim, CDs-AgNPs were aggregated and the ratio (A525 nm/A400 nm) became larger (Fig. 10b). These results indicated that the novel CDs-AgNPs probe had high specificity toward phoxim.

To access the applicability and accuracy of the novel developed CDs-AgNPs probe in real samples, environmental water (tap water and Yellow River) and fruit (Apple) sample were analyzed. They were spiked with different concentrations of phoxim and the ultimate concentration of phoxim can be calculated by the obtained regression equation. These results were summarized in Table 2, indicating novel developed CDs-AgNPs probe had good recoveries ranged from 87% to 110.0% with relative standard deviation (RSD) within 6.0%. Therefore, these results revealed the colorimetric sensor can be successfully used for the detection of phoxim with excellent reproducibility and accuracy in real samples.

In this work, green synthesis of CDs-AgNPs is employed to demonstrate a sensitive and reliable colorimetric determination of phoxim method. In the presence of phoxim, CDs-AgNPs were aggregated with color changed from yellow to red, which due to the SPR peak had red shift from 400 nm to 525 nm. The color changes can be observed with naked eyes. Recoveries were controlled in the range of 87–110.0%, the LOD of phoxim with UV–vis spectrometer was about 0.04 μM. This novel probe represents high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of phoxim in presence of other interferences, which reveals the CDs-AgNPs as a novel sensor was successfully used for monitoring phoxim in environmental water and fruit samples without sophisticated instrument, indicating this approach will benefit for various applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
